titleStmt.title: Morale and Home Intelligence Reports
Report: Morale Report for Period Sept to Nov 1944

div.note: LAND FORCES ADRIATIC
MORALE REPORT FOR PERIOD SEPT TO NOV. 1944
I. MORALE IN GENERAL.

In a force which has hitherto been mounting independent operations of comparatively short duration, morale fluctuates in direct proportion to the scale of operational activity. When operations are imminent or actually in progress a salutary keenness is apparent in all ranks who are connected, however remotely, with their preparation and execution. This was particularly noticeable after the liberation of GREECE, when all units were proud of the part they were able to play.

In general the standard of morale is high; the disappointment which became apparent when it was appreciated that hopes of the European War ending this year would not be realised, has been largely overcome.
II. FACTORS AFFECTING MORALE. hhh Discipline.

The monthly average of F.G.C.M. cases has been under 3, with a noticeable absence of grate offences; minor offences have been to a very large degree attributed to a situation which requires operational troops to live within easy reach of a large town. hhh Confidence.
(a). In army Leaders and the War. Uniformly high; past experience and possibly the Prime Minister's restraint have induced considerable reluctance to accept forecasts of 'target dates'.
(b). In Arms and Equipment. Very good; particularly in Specialist Units who have been able to select the most suitable equipment by trial and error.
(c). Units' confidence in themselves increases with every operation. hhh Mail.

There have been no complaints. Difficulty has been experienced in dealing satisfactorily with Task Forces made up of several sub units where no Army Postal Dot could be made available; a satisfactory solution has been reached. hhh Welfare and Entertainment.
(a). Living Conditions. All troops are billetted in covered accommodation when not on operation.

(b). Recreation and General Amusement. Central facilities in Bart are good, and accessible to many; VIS has its own mobile cinema. More travelling ENSA shows would be appreciated by isolated units. Outdoor recreation is universally popular; shortage of equipment makes considerable improvisation necessary.

(c). General. The eternal beer shortage is the only general complaint with NAAPI services, although it has been suggested that the extra Christmas fare should be a free issue. The demand for wireless sets, always great because of small detachments, is always greatly in excess of the supply. hhh Education with ABCA

Operational commitments and the fact that several units were widely scattered have prevented a set programme being closely followed. ABCA discussion form part of training programmes when possible. The visit of the SO III (Edn) to VIS was most successful, and great enthusiasm was shown by all units. War Office Correspondence Courses have been well received. hhh Relations between officers and men.
Always good.
All units have had practical experience of close cooperation with R.N. and R.A.F.; uniformly good relations are reported and confidence in the other services is high. hhh Relations with Allies.

The most important contact is with J.A.N.L. The universal admiration and respect for Yugoslavs which has been prevalent among units in VIS has not continued in those have recently been fighting on the mainland of Yugoslavia. Troops have been rudely shocked by conditions there, and, in spite of lectures, the British soldier finds it difficult to understand restrictions on relations with civilians, the harsh attitude adopted by J.A.N.L., towards every P.W. and their uncompromising treatment of all who are suspected of other political opinions. The feeling of mutual suspicion thus produced has had a considerable effect on morale. Relations with U.S.A. troops are potentially good and particularly so in a unit which has been working with Americans in enemy occupied territory. Different scales of pay and disciplinary standards remain a great bar to common understanding. hhh Home Affairs, Post War and Release.

The number of applications for compassionate posting to U.K. are increasing. It is difficult to make soldiers who are disturbed over family matters at home realise that the delay before their case is a decided is necessary in order to safeguard genuine cases, and there is a general feeling that marital infidelity should have a higher priority as a ground for home posting. Considerable interest is displayed in Post War planning - one of the more noticeable reactions being a doubt as to the many controls which will be imposed in order to implement the plans. The scheme for release form the army has been generally accepted. The suggestion that overseas service should be taken into account is a common one, but the almost insuperable difficulties of administration have been explained. The recent pay increases have disarmed criticism; it is, however, hard on those in receipt of War Service grants that there should be a corresponding decrease in their emoluments. Soldiers balance are notified now with much more despatch, although cynics insist that debit balances are notified much more quickly than credits. hhh Miscellaneous.

(a). Overseas Service. There is still, in spite of LIAP, a feeling that everything possible is not being done to reduce the necessary length of overseas service. Frequent changes in the reasons for its length, genuine though they may be, cannot but produce a measure of distrust.

(b). Leave LIAP has been a great blessing, and has given everyone new hope. It is unfortunate that by the time the allocation reaches a Regiment or Battalion the numbers appear ridiculously small. The manpower situation and other reasons for small drafts are well understood. Leave to ROME has at least been allowed for units of this Force, and is most welcome.
(c). Relations with civil population.
(i). Yugoslavia is covered in section 8.
(ii). It Italy no great fraternisation is reported. The expression 'correct aloofness' used by C.O. well sums up the general attitude.
[signature]
Brig.,
Comd. Land Forces Adriatic.
